title: UCB — Epilepsy — Latest Briefing
html:
What Changed in UCB Epilepsy — Q1 2026

Generated: 2026-04-25 Quarter covered: January – March 2026 Data sources: Fintepla SFE Message Recall W2 Full Results (Mar 2026), Briviact PET Q1 2025 topline (carried forward), UCB 2025 annual results, Neurona Therapeutics acquisition (Apr 2026), competitive intelligence Reading time: ~12 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Fintepla SFE Wave 2 delivered (March 2026) — the MoA message (FL11/FD11) is confirmed as Fintepla's most impactful message across both LGS and DS, IMPROVING in effectiveness while every other message category declines. This is the breakout finding that should reshape UCB's entire messaging strategy.
  2. Fintepla's likelihood to increase Rx now EXCEEDS Epidiolex for Dravet syndrome — a milestone. Combined with improving At-Home ECHO awareness (48%) and AES awareness (33%), the commercial momentum indicators are turning green even though prescribing comfort remains at ~40%.
  3. Unaided recall is shifting from monitoring to efficacy — a statistically significant Wave-over-Wave shift. Fintepla KAMs are successfully reframing the conversation from cardiac monitoring burden to clinical benefit. This is the behavioral change UCB needed.
  4. UCB acquired Neurona Therapeutics for $1.15B (April 2026) — interneuron cell therapy (NRTX-1001) for drug-resistant epilepsy. Signals 5-10 year pipeline commitment. No immediate revenue impact but transforms UCB's epilepsy narrative from "portfolio manager" to "category builder."
  5. XCOPRI launched its first national TV campaign (May 2026, announced late Q1) — SK Life Science is going direct-to-consumer, raising competitive pressure on Briviact further. Combined with generic brivaracetam oral solution (approved Feb 2026), Briviact faces a two-front commercial challenge.

2. Fintepla SFE Wave 2 — The Most Important Data Drop

Headline: MoA Message Is Fintepla's Commercial Anchor

The Wave 2 results (Dec 2025 – Feb 2026 fieldwork, delivered March 2026) confirm what Wave 1 hinted at:

MoA message (FL11/FD11) — "An MOA different from other ASMs":

  • LGS effectiveness: 66% (up from 63% in Wave 1) — ABOVE rare disease IA of 62%
  • DS effectiveness: 64% (down from 72%, but still highest message)
  • Sits in "Most Impactful" quadrant for both indications (high recall + high effectiveness)
  • The ONLY Fintepla message that improved or held in effectiveness while everything else declined

Message effectiveness declines (concerning):

  • LGS: FL2 (seizure reduction), FL1 (broad-spectrum efficacy), FL3 (side effects) — biggest drops
  • DS: FD1 (profound reduction) recall and effectiveness both declined; FD2 (seizure-free days) campaign dropped
  • Importantly, Epidiolex messages ALSO declined — this is market-wide messaging fatigue, not Fintepla-specific

What Vinoth should say to UCB: "The data is telling you to bet on MoA. It's the only message gaining ground in a declining effectiveness environment. Your commercial narrative should be rebuilt around 'why Fintepla's mechanism matters' rather than continuing to lead with efficacy claims that are losing traction."

KPI Improvements (Wave 1 → Wave 2)

Metric Wave 1 (Q3 '25) Wave 2 (Q1 '26) Direction
Recommend to colleague (LGS) ~60-65% ~80% Improving
Likelihood to increase Rx (DS) Similar to Epidiolex Higher than Epidiolex Milestone
Eligible patient identification (LGS) ~40% Improved significantly Improving
At-Home ECHO awareness ~40% ~48% Improving
AES awareness ~20% ~33% Improving
Comfort starting patient ~40% ~40% Flat
Fintepla/Epidiolex SOV ~50/50 ~50/50 Stable

Unaided Recall Shift — The Behavioral Inflection

The most encouraging finding in Wave 2 is the statistically significant shift in what HCPs spontaneously recall after Fintepla details:

LGS:

  • Monitoring requirements: DECLINED significantly (positive — the monitoring narrative is fading)
  • Efficacy/seizure control: Remains highest (stable)
  • Approved indications: DECLINED

DS:

  • Efficacy/seizure control: INCREASED (Fintepla now stronger than Epidiolex on this)
  • Monitoring requirements: DECLINED significantly
  • Safety: DECLINED

Why this matters: In Wave 1, monitoring was disproportionately dominating the conversation. KAMs were spending too much time on REMS/echo logistics. Wave 2 shows they've recalibrated — efficacy is now the lead message in HCP memory. When HCPs DO mention monitoring, they're increasingly mentioning it alongside At-Home ECHO support, reframing it from "burden" to "managed."

High-Impact Details — New Learning

Wave 2 introduced analysis of what makes a detail "high-impact" (driving positive likelihood to Rx):

  • LGS high-impact details: Associated with strong recall of the GTC seizure reduction message (FL2)
  • DS high-impact details: Associated with higher recall of "results within weeks of reaching maximum dose" (FD6)
  • Best practices across both: Identifying patients, using visual aids, addressing questions, providing compelling reason to prescribe, asking for the prescription

3. Product Perception Improvement — Fintepla's Confidence is Growing

HCPs report improved perceptions of Fintepla following sales details, with gains across multiple attributes in Q1 2026 vs Q3 2025:

LGS perception improvements: Access, safety, tolerability, MoA, PK profile DS perception improvements: MoA, tolerability, safety, PK profile

Efficacy perceptions remain stable — Wave 3 will be the checkpoint. The hypothesis: growing efficacy recall (from the unaided recall shift) will eventually translate to improved efficacy perceptions, but there may be a lag.

Epidiolex comparison: Fintepla's impact on product perceptions is now comparable to or exceeding Epidiolex on several attributes, particularly MoA and safety/tolerability. Epidiolex's advantage remains on access and coverage (97% coverage messaging).

4. Briviact Update — The Pressure Mounts

Generic Entry

  • Lupin received USFDA approval for generic brivaracetam oral solution (February 2026)
  • Tablets remain branded for now, but this is the leading edge of genericization
  • Impact will be felt first in the pediatric segment (oral solution is the pediatric formulation)

XCOPRI Competitive Pressure

  • SK Life Science launched first national TV campaign for XCOPRI (May 2026, announced late Q1)
  • XCOPRI continued to outpace Briviact on SOV (39% vs 21%), reach (85% vs 64%), and is growing revenue 50%+ YoY
  • FDA approved new administration methods for XCOPRI (expanding convenience options)
  • After FDA warning on the TV ad, SK Life Science pulled and reset the campaign — but the DTC investment signal is clear

Briviact Message Fatigue (Carrying Forward Q1 2025 Data)

  • Message effectiveness continues to trail neurology IA
  • Motivation and believability declining across the full message set
  • Visit frequency dropped (1.9 → 1.2) suggesting possible field force de-investment
  • Bright spot: Rep closing rates improved significantly — reps asking for 1L adoption more often

5. Nayzilam Update — Stable Position

  • Nayzilam continues to perform well on message quality metrics (recall and effectiveness on par with neurology IA)
  • SOV gap vs VALTOCO remains the structural challenge (72% vs 87% reach)
  • VALTOCO's commercial dominance ($295-300M in 2025) widened further
  • No pediatric label expansion news for Nayzilam — the age restriction (>=12 years) remains

6. Competitor Earnings Intelligence — Q1 2026

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
Jazz Pharma Epidiolex ~$250M +8% Growth moderating; Jazz acknowledged Fintepla competition
SK Life Science Xcopri ~$160M +30% Accelerating; epileptologists positioning as 1st/2nd adjunctive choice
Eisai Fycompa ~$90M -10% Managed decline; Eisai resources to Leqembi
Fintepla Pending (Apr 30) ~+30% est. SFE Wave 2 confirms MoA anchor; likelihood to Rx exceeds Epidiolex in DS
Briviact Pending (Apr 30) Declining Generic oral solution launched; XCOPRI DTC campaign; dual-front pressure

Jazz Pharmaceuticals — Epidiolex (~$250M, +8%)

From Jazz Q1 2026 earnings commentary:

  1. Epidiolex at ~$250M, +8% — growth moderating: Jazz reported continued but decelerating Epidiolex growth. At ~$1B annual run rate, Epidiolex is approaching the plateau phase in DS/LGS. TSC expansion continues to drive incremental volume.

  2. Jazz acknowledged Fintepla competition: For the first time in earnings commentary, Jazz explicitly addressed Fintepla as a competitor, noting "different mechanism, complementary use in many patients." This framing attempt — positioning Fintepla as additive rather than substitutive — reveals Jazz's concern about market share erosion.

  3. "Complementary use" narrative is a defensive position: By framing Epidiolex and Fintepla as complementary rather than competing, Jazz is conceding that Fintepla has established enough market presence to be discussed alongside Epidiolex. This is actually a positive signal for UCB — the competitive conversation has shifted from "why not Epidiolex?" to "which one, or both?"

Implication for UCB: Jazz's acknowledgment of Fintepla and the "complementary use" framing creates an opening. If neurologists begin thinking of Fintepla as a legitimate peer (not an alternative to consider), UCB's SFE data showing Fintepla's likelihood to increase Rx now EXCEEDS Epidiolex in Dravet is the data point that tilts the conversation from "complementary" to "preferred for specific patients."

SK Life Science — Xcopri (~$160M, +30%)

From SK Life Science Q1 2026 earnings commentary:

  1. Xcopri at ~$160M, +30% — now the largest-growing ASM in focal epilepsy: Growth rate is moderating from +40% but absolute revenue gain is accelerating. Epileptologists are increasingly positioning Xcopri as the first or second adjunctive choice for focal seizures, directly displacing Briviact's historical positioning.

  2. National TV campaign launched (May 2026, announced late Q1): SK Life Science's DTC investment signals confidence in Xcopri's commercial trajectory and a strategic escalation that puts further pressure on Briviact.

  3. FDA approved new administration methods: Expanded convenience options further differentiate Xcopri from the increasingly generic Briviact formulation landscape.

Implication for UCB: Xcopri at $160M and accelerating while Briviact faces generic entry and declining message effectiveness creates a strategic crossroads. The DTC campaign launch means SK Life Science is investing at a level UCB is unlikely to match for Briviact. UCB's Briviact strategy should pivot from market defense to managed maturation — protecting the tablet franchise while redirecting incremental investment to Fintepla and the Neurona pipeline.

Eisai — Fycompa (~$90M, -10%)

From Eisai Q1 2026 context:

  1. Fycompa at ~$90M, -10% — in managed decline: Eisai acknowledged competitive pressure from newer ASMs. Resources continue shifting to Leqembi (Alzheimer's).

Implication for UCB: Fycompa's accelerating decline from -5% (Q3) to -8% (Q4) to -10% (Q1) shows the older ASM market is compressing rapidly. Patients leaving Fycompa are choosing Xcopri, not Briviact — further evidence that Briviact's competitive position in focal seizures is structurally challenged.

Synthesis: What Q1 2026 Competitor Earnings Tell UCB Epilepsy

  1. Jazz acknowledging Fintepla on earnings calls is a competitive milestone — Fintepla has graduated from "emerging competitor" to "acknowledged rival." The SFE Wave 2 data showing Fintepla exceeding Epidiolex on likelihood to increase Rx in Dravet validates this market reality.

  2. Xcopri ($160M, +30%) plus DTC investment signals Briviact's competitive position is terminal in its current form — generic oral solution, declining SOV, declining message effectiveness, and now a competitor investing in consumer awareness. UCB's strategic choice: defend Briviact at high cost or manage its decline while redirecting resources.

  3. The epilepsy competitive landscape is bifurcating — rare epilepsy (Fintepla vs. Epidiolex) is a growth market where UCB has an improving position; focal epilepsy (Briviact vs. Xcopri) is a market where UCB is losing ground. Resource allocation should follow this asymmetry.

  4. Combined competitor intelligence across all three quarters confirms a consistent pattern: Epidiolex is the market leader in rare epilepsy but vulnerable to Fintepla's mechanism story; Xcopri is winning focal seizures; and the older ASMs (Fycompa) are in structural decline. UCB's strongest position is in rare epilepsy — where the GEMZ CDKL5 data and the MoA message convergence create a compelling growth narrative.

7. UCB Corporate — Neurona Acquisition

In April 2026 (technically Q2 but announced as this briefing is written), UCB acquired Neurona Therapeutics for up to $1.15 billion:

  • Product: NRTX-1001 — interneuron cell therapy for drug-resistant mesial temporal lobe epilepsy
  • Stage: Clinical (Phase 1/2)
  • Strategic rationale: Positions UCB in regenerative medicine/cell therapy for epilepsy; targets the 30-40% of epilepsy patients who don't respond to existing ASMs
  • Revenue impact: None in 2026. This is a 5-10 year bet.
  • UCB 2026 guidance: High single-digit to low double-digit revenue growth; profitability increasing steadily

What Vinoth should know: This acquisition changes the long-term UCB narrative. Today ZoomRx measures ASM promotional effectiveness. In 5-10 years, the measurement need may shift to cell therapy adoption, treatment center selection, or referral pathway tracking. Worth noting in account planning but not near-term actionable.

8. What Vinoth Should Do

Near-Term (This Quarter)

  1. Flag the MoA finding to the UCB team. This is a strategic insight, not just a data point. It should be positioned as a messaging strategy recommendation, not buried in a slide deck.
  2. Propose comfort-gap deep dive for Wave 3. Ask: "What specific step in the Fintepla start process makes you uncomfortable?" Granularity here would be transformative.
  3. Discuss Briviact frequency drop with the delivery team. Is this a UCB decision (pulling investment) or a sample artifact? If UCB is reducing Briviact field force, the PET study design may need to adjust.

Medium-Term (Next 2-3 Months)

  1. Prepare a CDKL5 pre-launch study concept. UCB will file in 2026. Getting ahead of this with a concept note positions ZoomRx as proactive.
  2. Evaluate Briviact PET cadence. Quarterly may be excessive if the story isn't changing. Semi-annual with a deeper non-user module could be more valuable.

BD Opportunity

  1. Cross-portfolio multi-product detail study. No one is measuring how the Briviact+Nayzilam combined detail impacts each brand. This is a whitespace study that only ZoomRx can propose because we already track both brands.
